lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Safely stowed.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Hamlet! Lord Hamlet!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But soft, what noise? who calls on Hamlet? O, here they come.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: What have you done, my lord, with the dead body?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Compounded it with dust, whereto ’tis kin.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Tell us where ’tis, that we may take it thence

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: And bear it to the chapel.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Do not believe it.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Believe what?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That I can keep your counsel and not mine own. Besides, to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: be demanded of a sponge! what replication should be made by the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: son of a king?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Take you me for a sponge, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, sir, that soaks up the king’s countenance, his rewards, his authorities.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But such officers do the king best service in the end: he

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: keeps them, like an ape, in the corner of his jaw; first

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: mouthed, to be last swallowed: when he needs what you have gleaned,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: it is but squeezing you, and, sponge, you shall be dry again.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: I understand you not, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am glad of it: a knavish speech sleeps in a foolish ear.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: My lord, you must tell us where the body is, and go

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: with us to the king.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The body is with the king, but the king is not with

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the body. The king is a thing—

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: A thing, my lord!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Of nothing: bring me to him. Hide fox, and all after.

glosses.definition: held in the mouth, swallowed last
a children's game (hide-and-seek): you find me now

annotations.title: 'A sponge that soaks up the king's countenance'
annotations.body: Hamlet's accusation of Rosencrantz — 'you shall be dry again' — uses one of the play's cleanest political metaphors. Courtiers are sponges: they absorb royal favor and are squeezed dry when the King has had his use of them. The image reduces the technique of flattery to a fluid mechanics. It is also a warning Rosencrantz cannot hear: Hamlet knows the King's ambassadors are disposable, as we will learn when the sealed commission turns out to contain their own death warrants. The short scene is otherwise almost entirely comic, the corpse-hide-and-seek played for grotesque laughter. Shakespeare allows himself this tonal variety because the tragedy's center has already tilted toward inevitability.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: 'The body is with the king, but the king is not with the body'
annotations.body: Hamlet's cryptic reply to Rosencrantz — about Polonius's body, addressed to Claudius — is more than madman's evasion. 'The body is with the king, but the king is not with the body' plays simultaneously on the medieval doctrine of the king's two bodies (the immortal body politic and the mortal body natural) and on the fact that Polonius's corpse is somewhere in the castle but the King it served is no longer honored by it. The phrase 'a thing, my lord... of nothing' prefigures the meditations on death and dust in the graveyard scene. Hamlet's madness here is intellectual — each line a small riddle the audience is asked to decode.
sources: Ernst Kantorowicz, The King's Two Bodies (1957)

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet mean by calling Rosencrantz a 'sponge'?
options: That he dries up quickly under pressure.
That he absorbs the King's favor and will be squeezed dry when no longer useful.
That he is soft and easily destroyed.
That he is dishonorable like an old wet rag.

trials.prompt: What is Hamlet's state in this scene?
options: Truly mad and unable to speak coherently.
Feigning madness while using riddles to insult his captors and elude their questions.
Preparing to surrender himself to the King's justice.

trials.prompt: What does the refrain 'Hide fox, and all after' invoke?
options: A hunting call used by Danish nobility.
A children's game of hide-and-seek, casting Hamlet as the fox being chased.
A nursery rhyme about Reynard the Fox.
